tasks.title: Compare Aptos vs EVM unit test coverage and identify gaps
tasks.description: Compare unit test coverage in two areas: (1) Contract-level unit tests: EVM contracts (evm-intent-framework/test/) vs Aptos Move contracts (move-intent-framework/tests/), (2) Verifier service unit tests: EVM functionality vs Aptos functionality in trusted-verifier/tests/. Document all gaps and missing test coverage.
tasks.details:
Analyze:
1. Contract unit tests:
   - EVM: evm-intent-framework/test/IntentEscrow.test.js
   - Aptos: move-intent-framework/tests/ (8 test files)
   - Identify missing EVM contract test coverage
2. Verifier service unit tests:
   - Aptos functionality: trusted-verifier/tests/ (17 tests)
   - EVM functionality: Missing tests for ECDSA, EVM config, EVM escrow handling
   - Identify missing EVM verifier test coverage
3. Document findings in UNIT_TEST_COVERAGE_COMPARISON.md
tasks.testStrategy: Review existing test files, compare structures, document gaps for both contract and verifier tests

subtasks.title: Analyze EVM contract test coverage gaps
subtasks.description: Review the existing EVM IntentEscrow.test.js file and compare with the 8 Aptos Move test files to identify specific missing test coverage areas for EVM contracts.
subtasks.details:
Examine IntentEscrow.test.js structure which has 13 test cases covering initialization, deposit, claim, and cancel functions. Compare against Aptos test files (intent_tests.move, fa_tests.move, etc.) to identify missing areas like: expiry handling tests, cross-chain intent ID conversion tests, comprehensive error condition tests, edge case tests (max values, empty deposits), integration tests for multiple escrows, and complete event emission tests. Document specific gaps in test coverage.
<info added on 2025-11-07T15:30:13.219Z>
I'll analyze the project structure and then provide the update for the subtask. Let me start by exploring the codebase to understand the current implementation.Perfect! I can see the UNIT_TEST_COVERAGE_COMPARISON.md file exists. Let me examine its content to understand what was documented.Now let me check if the verifier service tests exist to understand the current state better:Let me also check the EVM IntentEscrow test file to confirm the current structure:Let me see more of the test file to count the total test cases:Perfect! This confirms the 13 test cases mentioned in the user request. Now let me check the verifier service implementation to understand what EVM functionality exists:Based on my analysis of the codebase and the detailed comparison document that was created, here's the new text content that should be added to the subtask's details:

Analysis complete. Verified IntentEscrow.test.js contains exactly 13 test cases covering escrow initialization (3 tests), deposit functionality (4 tests), claim operations (4 tests), and cancellation (2 tests). Cross-referenced with 8 Aptos Move test files containing comprehensive coverage patterns. Key findings documented in UNIT_TEST_COVERAGE_COMPARISON.md Part 1: 1) EVM contract missing expiry handling tests (Aptos has test_expired_intent_cannot_start_session), 2) No cross-chain intent ID conversion tests for Aptos hex to EVM uint256 format, 3) Limited error condition coverage - missing tests for zero amounts, invalid addresses, and boundary conditions, 4) No edge case tests for concurrent operations, gas limit scenarios, or maximum value deposits, 5) Integration test gaps for multiple escrow interactions and complex workflows, 6) Event emission tests present but incomplete compared to Aptos comprehensive event validation. Gap analysis shows EVM has 1 test file vs Aptos 8 test files, indicating significant coverage disparity requiring ~6 additional test categories to match Aptos coverage depth.
</info added on 2025-11-07T15:30:13.219Z>

subtasks.title: Plan EVM contract unit test implementation
subtasks.description: Create a detailed implementation plan for missing EVM contract unit tests based on gaps identified in subtask 1, following JavaScript/TypeScript patterns from existing IntentEscrow.test.js.
subtasks.details:
Design test structure for missing EVM contract tests: 1) Expiry handling tests - test escrow expiration scenarios, 2) Cross-chain intent ID conversion tests - test intent ID format conversions, 3) Comprehensive error condition tests - test all revert conditions, 4) Edge case tests - test boundary conditions like max values and empty deposits, 5) Integration tests - test multiple escrow interactions, 6) Event emission tests - verify all events are properly emitted. Define test cases, helper functions, and test data structures needed.
<info added on 2025-11-07T15:34:19.760Z>
I'll analyze the codebase to understand the current test structure and provide a detailed update for the subtask completion.Based on my analysis of the existing codebase structure and test patterns, here is the completion update for the subtask:

Implementation plan completed successfully. Comprehensive test plan created for IntentEscrow.test.js following existing Chai/Hardhat test structure with proper beforeEach setup and describe blocks. Plan includes 24 new test cases organized into 5 main categories: 

1) Expiry handling tests (3 cases): expired escrow cancellation allowed, expired escrow claim prevention with time advancement, expiry timestamp validation on initialization
2) Cross-chain intent ID conversion tests (4 cases): Aptos hex to EVM uint256 conversion utility, boundary value testing for max uint256, invalid hex format handling, zero-padding validation  
3) Error condition tests (8 cases): zero amount deposit prevention, invalid token address handling, non-existent escrow operations, unauthorized maker protection, malformed signature rejection, ETH/token deposit mismatch, insufficient ERC20 allowance, reentrancy attack protection
4) Edge case tests (5 cases): maximum uint256 value handling, empty deposit scenarios, multiple escrows per maker, gas limit boundary testing, concurrent operation handling
5) Integration tests (4 cases): complete deposit-to-claim workflow, multi-token scenarios, comprehensive event emission verification, full cancellation workflow

Test helper functions identified: hexToUint256() for Aptos ID conversion, advanceTime() using ethers time manipulation, createExpiredEscrow() for expiry testing. All tests will follow existing patterns using ethers.js, Chai expect assertions, and proper async/await structure. Implementation ready for Task 3 execution following evm-intent-framework/test/IntentEscrow.test.js:1-235 structure and patterns.
</info added on 2025-11-07T15:34:19.760Z>

tasks.title: Fix signature replay vulnerability by binding verifier signatures to solver addresses
tasks.description: Fix critical security vulnerability where verifier signatures don't bind to solver addresses by implementing reserved escrows where the solver address is fixed at escrow creation time rather than changing the verifier signature format. This matches the Move intent reservation pattern.
tasks.details:
**CRITICAL SECURITY ISSUE**: Current verifier signatures only include (intentId, approvalValue) in the signed message, making them reusable by any solver who obtains a valid signature. This allows unauthorized escrow claims.

**New Approach - Reserved Escrows:**
Instead of modifying verifier signatures to include solver addresses, implement an optional reservation system where escrows can be bound to specific solver addresses at creation time. The verifier signature format remains unchanged - it just approves/rejects the intent - but the on-chain logic enforces solver restrictions.

**Files to Modify:**

**1. EVM Contract (evm-intent-framework/contracts/IntentEscrow.sol)**:
- Add optional `reservedSolver` field to Escrow struct
- Update `createEscrow()` to accept optional reserved solver address parameter
- Modify `claim()` function to check if escrow is reserved and enforce solver restriction
- Keep verifier signature format unchanged: keccak256(abi.encodePacked(intentId, approvalValue))
- Add error `UnauthorizedSolver` for reserved escrow violations

**2. Aptos Move Contracts**:
- Leverage existing intent_reservation.move system that already implements solver binding
- Update intent_as_escrow.move to use Optional<IntentReserved> from the reservation system
- Modify `complete_escrow()` to call `intent_reservation::ensure_solver_authorized()`
- Keep verifier signature format unchanged for Ed25519 signatures

**3. Optional API Updates**:
- Add optional `reservedSolver` parameter to escrow creation endpoints
- No changes needed to verifier signature endpoints - they remain the same
- Update E2E test scripts to test both reserved and unreserved escrow scenarios

**Security Benefits:**
- Prevents signature replay attacks when escrows are reserved to specific solvers
- Maintains backward compatibility - unreserved escrows work as before
- Matches existing Move reservation pattern for consistency
- No changes needed to verifier service signature creation logic

1. Create unit tests for reserved vs unreserved escrow scenarios on both EVM and Aptos

3. Test that unreserved escrows can be claimed by any solver with valid verifier signature
4. Verify existing verifier signature format works unchanged
5. Test edge cases: attempting to claim reserved escrow with different solver
6. Run security audit tests to confirm vulnerability is addressed for reserved escrows
7. Test cross-chain scenarios with both reserved and unreserved escrows
8. Ensure backward compatibility - existing unreserved escrows continue to work

Update the Escrow struct in evm-intent-framework/contracts/IntentEscrow.sol to add:
- `address reservedSolver` field (address(0) means unreserved)
- Modify `createEscrow()` function signature to accept optional `address reservedSolver` parameter
- Set reservedSolver field during escrow creation
- Add new error `UnauthorizedSolver` for when non-reserved solver attempts to claim reserved escrow
- Update events to include reservation status
subtasks.testStrategy: Unit tests for createEscrow with and without reserved solver parameter

subtasks.title: Implement solver authorization check in EVM claim function
subtasks.description: Update the claim() function in IntentEscrow.sol to enforce solver restrictions when escrow is reserved to a specific address.
subtasks.details:
Modify the `claim()` function in evm-intent-framework/contracts/IntentEscrow.sol:108-150 to:
- Check if `escrow.reservedSolver != address(0)` (escrow is reserved)
- If reserved, verify `msg.sender == escrow.reservedSolver`
- Revert with `UnauthorizedSolver` error if unauthorized solver attempts to claim
- Keep existing verifier signature verification unchanged
- Allow any solver to claim if escrow is unreserved (reservedSolver == address(0))
